source.title: Emergence of abstract rules in the primate brain.

confidence_rationale: Three-lab synthesis (Mansouri / Freedman / Buckley) at NRN, directly framed around 'dynamic emergence of abstract rules in primate cognition, and the distributed neural network that supports abstract rule formation, maintenance, revision, and task-dependent implementation.' The review is the strongest existing source for the position that rule representations are NOT innate -- they form during training and are shaped by experience. For SD-033a A2 specifically (on what training signal is the rule-bias projection shaped?), the review supports the trained-head alternative against the frozen-random landing -- biology grows the substrate, it does not start from a randomly-initialised projection that never updates. However, the review does NOT specify a unique training signal. It documents emergence under reinforcement learning paradigms, supervised category-judgement paradigms, and matching-rule paradigms with very different feedback structures, and notes that representational features (proportions, latencies, generalisation) vary across these training regimes. The mixed direction reflects this: clear support for trainable emergence, no commitment to which training signal SD-033a should use. Confidence held at 0.78 because the source quality is very high (NRN, three-lab review, directly on-topic) but the mapping to SD-033a's specific bias-projection-head architecture is moderate (the review describes lateral-PFC rule emergence at the population level, not the projection-onto-action-selection level that score_bias represents). The existing PFC-subdivision-architecture entry on this same paper (2026-04-19, MECH-261/ARC-035) covered the rule-substrate location question; this entry covers the orthogonal training-signal-emergence question and is appropriately tagged to SD-033a + MECH-262.
failure_signatures: If rule-selective neurons could be shown to be present at proportions and latencies indistinguishable from trained levels in NAIVE animals before any task experience, the dynamic-emergence framing would collapse and a frozen-random head with no training would become biologically grounded rather than a backward-compatibility shim. The review surveys substantial evidence against this scenario but explicit naive-vs-trained dissociation studies are sparse outside specific paradigms.

The review reports rule emergence under multiple training regimes (delayed match-to-sample, category judgement, conditional rule-shifting, WCST analogues). If different regimes produce qualitatively different representational features, then SD-033a's choice of phased-training protocol determines what kind of bias head emerges. A 'just train the head with whatever signal' approach risks producing a biologically anomalous representation that does not match any specific training regime.

The review does not adjudicate between dopaminergic / RPE-driven training of the projection itself versus Hebbian / co-activation strengthening shaped by population-level reward modulation. If the projection is shaped purely by Hebbian dynamics under a global neuromodulatory gate (i.e. the projection itself does not carry a per-trial gradient), SD-033a's trained-head alternative would need a substantially different implementation than backprop-with-RL-target.
Review-level synthesis rather than direct experimental test. Confidence is in what the field collectively reports, not in any single replicated finding.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Abstract-rule-selective neurons in primate lateral PFC emerge through training across paradigms (delayed match-to-sample, category judgement, conditional rule-shifting, WCST analogues). The proportion of rule-selective cells, the latency of rule-driven activity, and the breadth of generalisation all develop as the animal learns the task. A distributed network spanning lateral PFC, OFC, and ACC supports rule formation, maintenance, revision, and task-dependent implementation, with each region contributing distinct dynamics. The review does not assign a single biological training signal to the rule-bias substrate.
mapping.ree_translation: SD-033a's A2 question is whether the score_bias head should remain a frozen-random projection (the landing choice, used here purely for backward-compatibility) or be trained via a phased protocol on a specific signal. Mansouri, Freedman & Buckley argue that biological rule substrates emerge dynamically through training -- they are not innate, and their representational features depend on the training regime. This favours the trained-head alternative against an indefinitely-frozen head: a SD-033a that never trains its bias projection cannot, by construction, instantiate signature (iv) of MECH-262 (training-dependent emergence). What this review does NOT supply is the specific signal: it does not adjudicate between reward-prediction-error signals propagated through corticostriatal loops, supervised rule-label gradients, and Hebbian co-activation shaped by global neuromodulatory gates. The training signal question therefore remains open even after this review; what the review settles is the prior question of whether to train the head AT ALL.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The review describes lateral-PFC rule emergence at the level of single-unit population features (proportions, latencies, persistence). SD-033a's score_bias is a low-dimensional parametric projection from rule_state to a per-candidate scalar, added to dacc_score_bias before E3.select(). The architectural correspondence is approximate -- biological rule emergence shapes the representation in lateral PFC, while SD-033a's trained-head alternative would shape a downstream projection-onto-action-selection. These are correlated but not identical processes. The transfer assumption is that 'rule representation emerges with training' implies 'projections from rule representation to action selection also emerge with training', which the review supports indirectly via the network-distributed-emergence framing but does not test directly. The review also focuses on macaque single-unit data; transfer to a humanised V3 agent inherits standard non-human-primate-to-human caveats.
mapping.source_context: Nature Reviews Neuroscience (2020, vol 21:595-610), three-lab synthesis (Monash / Chicago / Oxford), single-unit recording + lesion + imaging studies in primates, abstract-rule-guided behaviour paradigms.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality very high (NRN, three-lab review with deep methodological coverage). Mapping fidelity is moderate -- the review supports trainable emergence at the substrate level but does not address the specific projection-head training-signal question SD-033a A2 poses. Transfer risk moderate because the review supports the prior (rules are trained) but leaves the specific signal underdetermined, so any V3 implementation choice will inherit residual ambiguity until a more focused training-signal study lands.
